questions.description: Ken is a dairy farmer with a 5 years lease on a 20 acre piece of farm… Advise Ken.
questions.text: Ken is a dairy farmer with a 5 years lease on a 20 acre piece of farm land, consisting of fields and animal sheds with machines to milk the cows. When he entered into the lease he explained to the landlord, Mary, that he had chosen this land as it was "so close to the local dairy" that it would be perfect for his dairy business and would greatly reduce his transport costs. However, one year into the lease there was an unprecedented outbreak of disease in livestock in the local area. This was declared an animal health crisis, and all farms in the area, including Ken's, were closed down for a period of 6 months by the local health authority. During this time, Ken had no income from his cow herd and, although a small amount of compensation was paid by the local health authority, he was unable to pay the rent on the lease. Mary is threatening to sue Ken for the rent owed for the 6 months his farm was closed, and Ken wants to know if he has any defence to her claim for rent. In addition, the public health crisis meant that the local dairy was forced to close. This means that Ken now has to travel a much farther distance to sell the milk, making his business economically unviable for the future. Although there are no clauses in the contract dealing with the possibility of early termination of the lease, Ken is considering terminating his lease early, and wants to know if he might have any grounds for doing so. Advise Ken.

questions.description: Dervla is a freelance graphic designer. Breach Of Contract.
questions.text: Dervla is a freelance graphic designer. She enters into a contract with a national newspaper to design the advertisements for the weekend edition of its newspaper, for a period of 2 years. During negotiations, she states that the nature of the job means that she will be able to do it from home. The newspaper editor says "Of course! We wouldn't expect you to come into the office!" However, six months into the contract, the newspaper editor changes. The new editor says that he wants Dervla to come into the newspaper offices every Thursday to do the design work, instead of working from home. When Dervla says that she cannot do so, he terminates the contract "for breach of contract". Dervla is furious, as she is losing out on a major source of income. She wants to know if she has any cause of action in contract law. Advise Dervla.

questions.description: Coldstore Ltd is a transporter of frozen food and pharmaceuticals to… Damages, Penalty Clause.
questions.text: Coldstore Ltd is a transporter of frozen food and pharmaceuticals to businesses across Ireland, with its own fleet of refrigerated trucks to make deliveries. In January 2021, Coldstore Ltd agreed to be a transporter for a large pharmaceutical company, Xyn Ltd. Tom, the manager of Coldstore Ltd, signed the pharmaceutical company's standard form contract. In December 2021, one of the deliveries was two days late due to a shortage of drivers. Xyn Ltd claims that, as a result of this delay, one of their most profitable production lines, producing Covid vaccines for the Government, was held up, losing them profits of €1 million. Xyn Ltd is seeking damages for lost profits, based on the following clause of its contract with Coldstore Ltd: "In the event that any delivery is more than 24 hours late, the transporter shall be responsible for the costs of all and any delays in production, including all lost profits, and an additional payment of 150% of the cost of the material transported shall be immediately due." Coldstore Ltd believes that this is a penalty clause and should not be enforceable. In addition, Coldstore Ltd says that they had no way of knowing that that particular delivery was so important and would result in this extent of lost profits if delayed. Advise Coldstore Ltd.

questions.description: Carla is a dance teacher who has recently set up a dance school, StarShine. Negligenc.
questions.text: Carla is a dance teacher who has recently set up a dance school, StarShine. She also has a website on which she provides information about her classes and sells merchandise such as dance clothes, ballet shoes and equipment. Before her students take their first class she asks them (or their parents) to provide their details and sign a document that she wrote using a template she found on the internet. The document contains the following clauses: "1. All classes are taken at students' own risk. Starshine is not liable for any personal injury which is caused during our classes, even if caused by negligence of Starshine instructors or employees. All statutory warranties and conditions are excluded. 2. The class fee for the term, specified overleaf, must be paid in full before term begins, and is non-refundable, including in the event that a class is cancelled for any reason. An administrative fee of €30 is payable if payment is not made on time." She is now worried about the validity of these clauses and seeks your advice. In addition, a pair of ballet shoes purchased on her website was recently returned by a customer who claimed that "they didn't fit", even though the shoes were the size stated on the website. She says that on her website it is clearly stated that she has a "no returns unless faulty" policy, and wants to know if she has to refund this customer, who returned the goods one week after purchase. Advise Carla.

questions.description: Fiona is a landscape gardener, who specialises in designing, building and… Advise Fiona.
questions.text: Fiona is a landscape gardener, who specialises in designing, building and maintaining gardens and outdoor areas for businesses such as hotels. Fiona receives a phone call from a local mushroom grower, Mark, asking if Fiona would be interested in taking a truck full of mushroom compost for use in her business, as otherwise he "would just be throwing it out". Fiona is delighted and immediately agrees to take the compost. When she collects the compost the next day Fiona is so happy with it that she promises she will pay Mark €200. Mark is delighted by this; however, Fiona never pays him the money. Mark is now insisting that she owes him the €200. During the Covid pandemic, Fiona realises that she needs additional financial support if the business is to succeed. She enters into an agreement with Thomas, a local entrepreneur who sells plants, bushes and gardening equipment. Thomas agrees to invest €30,000 in Fiona's business in return fir a promise that Fiona will enter into a contract with him for the supply of plants and gardening equipment to her business for the next year.However, after two weeks of discussions, they fail to agree on important terms of the supply contract, including the price and delivery arrangements. Fiona wants to end negotiations, but Thomas is insisting that she is under a contractual duty to negotiate the contract and/or has a duty of good faith to negotiate. Advise Fiona.

questions.description: Ross is the manager of a construction site. Advise Ross.
questions.text: Ross is the manager of a construction site. He needs to hire a large excavator for the site. On Monday he emails Trina, the owner of "Exc Hire Ltd" to enquire about the price of hiring an "XL Excavator" for six months. That afternoon, Trina responds as follows: "Thank you for your enquiry. We have one XL Excavator in stock, at a price of €3,000 per month plus delivery charges. If interested, please fill out the attached order form and return within 3 working days." On Wednesday Ross replies, saying "That's great, I'll take it!" However, he does not include the order form with his email. Trina replies to tell him that she hired out the XL Excavator to somebody else on Tuesday and so she cannot hire it to Ross. Ross is angry because he cannot find the XL Excavator for hire at this price anywhere else, and he believes he has a binding contract with Trina. Advise Ross. Would your answer be different if Ross had filled in and returned the order form as requested?

questions.description: Shady Ltd operates a large manufacturing plant producing a range of… Company, Director.
questions.text: Shady Ltd operates a large manufacturing plant producing a range of popular breakfast cereals. Shady Ltd was recently invoiced by its accountants, Audit Ltd, for €14,000 for audit services provided. Shady Ltd's managing director, Tom, has seen a rumour on social media that Audit Ltd is in tough financial situation and is at risk of becoming insolvent. Deciding to take advantage of this, he tells Audit ltd that unless the quoted figure of €14,000 is substantially reduced, he "won't pay them a penny" and he will personally see to it that no company in the country will ever hire them again. Audit Ltd agrees to a payment of €10,000 but subsequently sends a letter seeking the full €14,000 due, arguing that the agreement to pay €10,000 is void for duress. Advise Shady Ltd. Are there any other legal doctrines that Audit Ltd could potentially rely on, in addition to duress?

questions.description: Charm Ltd is a firm of computer software engineers, which specialises in… Company.
questions.text: Charm Ltd is a firm of computer software engineers, which specialises in providing protection to businesses against cyber-attacks, malicious software and online viruses and scams. In September 2021, Charm Ltd entered into a 5-year contract with Dart Ltd, to provide computer protection and maintenance services for a monthly fee of €10,000. The contract states (amongst other things): "Term 5: Charm Ltd will use its best endeavours to provide and ensure continuous supply of protection against cyber-attacks and malicious software for the duration of the agreement." However, on three separate occasions in December 2021 Dart Ltd was the victim of small- scale cyber-attacks. On each occasion the harm was quickly discovered and rectified, but Dart Ltd is now concerned that Charm Ltd may not be able to keep them safe. Dart Ltd has said that it wants to terminate its contract with Charm Ltd based on a breach of Term 5. In January 2022 Charm Ltd entered into a 2 year contract with another company, Arrow Ltd, to provide a similar range of services. The contract with Arrow Ltd was to begin in May 2022, with the first monthly payment due that month. However, in February 2022 Arrow Ltd purported to cancel the contract, saying that they were entitled to do so as performance had not begun and they had given "reasonable notice" approximately two months before the first payment was due. Advise Charm Ltd on both contracts.

questions.description: Mega Ltd sells equipment and machinery to the farms and agricultural… Breach Of Contract.
questions.text: Mega Ltd sells equipment and machinery to the farms and agricultural businesses. In January 2020 Mega Ltd entered into a 5 year transport agreement with Trucks Ltd, under which Trucks Ltd agreed to deliver machinery and equipment to Mega Ltd's customers at a fixed rate of €5 per kilometre. However, by August 2022, unanticipated high rates of inflation and an extraordinary rise in fuel prices had greatly increased the cost to Trucks Ltd of transporting the goods. Trucks Ltd says it needs to increase its rate to €8 a kilometre, as it says that it cannot continue to suffer losses transporting the goods. Trucks Ltd says that if Mega Ltd does not agree to the increase in price that they will be forced to terminate the contract. Mega Ltd seeks your advice on whether Trucks Ltd can increase its rates in this manner and/or terminate the contract. In addition, in January 2022 Mega Ltd entered into a contract for the sale of machinery worth €500,000 to a large farm in Ruritania, with €50,000 being paid in advance. However, Ruritania has since become involved in an international conflict and is the subject of sanctions issued by the EU which mean that it is now illegal to trade with any business entity in Ruritania in a number of sectors, including agriculture. Mega Ltd has contacted the buyers in Ruritania to say that they cannot go ahead with the contract. The buyers have threatened to sue for breach of contract and are also looking for the immediate return of the €50,000 paid in advance. Advise Mega Ltd. In your answer, assume that the contracts are governed by Irish law.

questions.description: Sarah owns a horse riding school. Advise Sarah.
questions.text: Sarah owns a horse riding school. In May 2022 she saw the following advertisement for a horse on the website "GreatDeal.ie": "Quiet grey horse for sale. 9 years old, in good health. Great with children and nervous riders. Private seller." Sarah contacted Tom, the owner of the horse, and arranged a viewing. Sarah explained that she was interested in the horse to use in her riding school with children and beginners. During the viewing the horse became excited and bucked, but Tom stated that it would "quickly settle into any new environment". When Sarah suggested that she would have a vet examine the horse, Tom said there was "no need" as he had had "no issues at all". Sarah bought the horse. However, even after a settling period of a few weeks it became clear that the horse was not quiet, and would try to buck off even experienced riders. In addition, the horse quickly developed a limp and when Sarah brought it to the vets the vet said that the horse had a pre• existing injury which was causing the limp. The horse was entirely unsuitable for Sarah's business and she wants to return it and get her money back. However, Tom says that Sarah had an opportunity to view the horse and took it "as seen". In addition, he says that as he is a private seller, and not operating in the course of business, she has no rights against him. Advise Sarah. Would your answer be any different if Tom was acting in the course of business rather than as a private seller?

questions.description: In January 2019, Niamh rents an office from Clumbo Ltd, agreeing to pay an… Company.
questions.text: In January 2019, Niamh rents an office from Clumbo Ltd, agreeing to pay an annual rent of €58,000 subject to a rent review in June 2022. The rent review clause provides that on that date the rent should be increased to the current market rental value as agreed between the parties. In June 2022, Max, a senior manager in Clumbo Ltd, writes to Niamh suggesting a figure of €57,000 for the new rent. Niamh writes back readily agreeing to renew upon the terms proposed. Max then realises his mistake and writes to Niamh explaining that he had intended to propose a rent of €75,000. Niamh informs Max that she will not agree to the correction proposed and that she considers that a binding agreement exists. While accepting that Max has authority to act on its behalf, Clumbo Ltd argues that Max's mistake invalidates the agreement. Clumbo Ltd is unhappy with Max's performance and it decides his annual salary of €90,000 could be put to better use. In July 2022, Max is dismissed and Clumbo Ltd pays him compensation of €150,000. However it is subsequently discovered that Max has engaged in insider dealing in breach of his service contract and in breach of company law. It becomes clear thus that at the time it terminated his contract, Clumbo Ltd could have dismissed Max for breach without having to pay compensation. Clumbo Ltd argues that the termination contract is void and the compensation repayable. Advise Clumbo Ltd. 1

questions.description: Becky is the owner of a small hotel. Regulation.
questions.text: Becky is the owner of a small hotel. She needs the light and electricity sockets in multiple rooms fixed, and as she is on a tight budget she asks the local electrician, Mark, if he can "do a deal". Mark admits that he is not a Registered Electrical Contractor, but he will do the job cheaply for Becky if she pays him cash in hand in advance. Becky pays Mark €1,000, but Mark only completes the work in half of the rooms. In addition, some of the electricity sockets are not working properly and Becky is worried that these might be a fire hazard. Becky contacts Mark to get her money back, and to ask him to fix the damaged sockets. Mark says that as it is an offence under the Electricity Regulation Act 1999 for any individual to carry out electrical works unless they are a Registered Electrical Contractor the contract is illegal and void. He refuses to pay back Becky the money she paid him. Advise Becky.

questions.description: Dervla is a graphic designer who is concerned about health issues, in… Advise Dervla.
questions.text: Dervla is a graphic designer who is concerned about health issues, in particular the dangers of young people smoking. On her own initiative she designs a poster for "Tobac", a charity that works to educate young people about the dangers of smoking. Edward, the public relations officer for Tobac, is delighted with the poster, and in an email to Dervla he writes: "We love your poster! As a token of our gratitude for your hard work, we would like to give you €1,000." Dervla replies "That's great, thank you!" However, the next week Edward emails Dervla to say that owing to cutbacks they are no longer able to pay her any money. Dervla wants to know if there is any way she can claim the €1,000 promised. Dervla discovers that her 15 year old niece Anna has started smoking. Dervla tells Anna that if she quits smoking and does not smoke for six months that she will pay her €500. Six months later Anna contacts Dervla to let her know that she has successfully abstained from smoking for six months and that she wants her money. Dervla argues that she wasn't serious when she offered Anna the money; that she has not benefited from Anna's actions; and that it was only a promise to a young family member and not legally binding. She wants to know if she has to pay Anna the €500. Advise Dervla.

questions.description: Brian is the manager of Viewplex Cinema, a cinema in Galway. Advise Andy.
questions.text: Brian is the manager of Viewplex Cinema, a cinema in Galway. On 1st March 2022, Brian entered into a contract with Andy, the manager of the local leisure centre and swimming pool, to display advertisements for the leisure centre in the Viewplex Cinema building for a period of one year. Under the terms of the contract, Viewplex Cinema is to display the advertisements for one year from 2nd May 2022, and Andy is to pay in one lump sum of €5,000 at the end of the agreed period. On 2nd April 2022, Andy tells Brian that he no longer wants to use Viewplex Cinema for advertisements, because he has found another location willing to display the advertisements at a lower price. However, Brian insists on continuing with the contract and displays the advertisements in the cinema from 2nd May as stipulated in the contract. Brian says that he is going to perform the contract as agreed and claim the full lump sum at the end of the year. Andy is furious, as he says that Brian could easily find another business owner willing to display advertisements in the cinema. He says that Brian should not be able to claim the full €5,000 due as he has failed to mitigate his losses. Advise Andy.

questions.description: Sophie is the owner of a restaurant. Breach Of Contract, Damages.
questions.text: Sophie is the owner of a restaurant. With the increased business after the easing of Covid restrictions she decides to expand her business premises. In January 2022, she hires Pat to build a fully completed extension onto the restaurant, including a bigger kitchen and washroom. Pat then enters into a sub-contract with Jim, a local plumber, to carry out the plumbing work on the new extension. The contract between Sophie and Pat clearly states that the work was to be finished by May 31st 2022. However, owing to staff shortages there are significant delays in finishing the work and Sophie is only able to open up part of her restaurant during the busy tourist season. Sophie has lost out on the increased profits she was expecting on completion of the extension, and wants to know if she can claim damages. She notes that her contract seems to allow for this possibility, as clause 12 states: "Time is of the essence of this contract. If the contractor fails to complete its work by the date specified, then the contractor hereby agrees to pay to the client, as liquidated clauses? Is this protection adequate or in need of reform? Refer to relevant case law and legislation in your answer damages and not as penalty, the sum of €500 per day for each calendar day beyond the specified completion date during which the contractor fails to complete the work." Sophie says that she used a template contract she found on the internet, but that she changed the sum to €500 as she felt that was a fair estimation of what she would lose if the work was delayed. Pat is arguing that he was unaware of the term, and says it is an unfair contract term and not enforceable. In addition, when the work is complete Sophie is unhappy with the quality of some of Jim's plumbing work. She says that the water pressure is too low for the dishwashers to work effectively and that there is a major leak from one of the sinks. She wants to sue Jim for breach of contract. Advise Sophie.

questions.description: How are Irish consumers protected from unfair contract terms and exclusion
questions.text: How are Irish consumers protected from unfair contract terms and exclusion
